item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Post
item.content:encoded: Sounds good to me!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author approved of edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

I feel like this could benefit from a link to Project Heimdall, considering that its containment requires extraterrestrial involvement.

Editing this:

Various space observatories have been co-opted by the Foundation to determine the location of possible SCP-2735 artifacts in solar orbit. In the event of an SCP-2735 artifact being discovered, a standard Foundation retrieval team will be sent into orbit to recover the object(s). Under the unlikely circumstances that there is a surviving SCP-2735 instance onboard an object, that object is to be transferred into an orbit around earth and de-orbited, so the surviving instance can be relocated to the primary SCP-2735 containment unit.

To this:

Containment of extraterrestrial satellites related to SCP-2735 has been delegated to Project Heimdall operations. Various space observatories have been co-opted by the Foundation to determine the location of possible SCP-2735 artifacts in solar orbit. In the event of an SCP-2735 artifact being discovered, a standard Foundation retrieval team will be sent into orbit to recover the object(s). Under the unlikely circumstances that there is a surviving SCP-2735 instance onboard an object, that object is to be transferred into an orbit around earth and de-orbited, so the surviving instance can be relocated to the primary SCP-2735 containment unit.

item.title: Re: From the article....
item.content:encoded:
Technically the flags on the moon are currently extraterrestrial, but were made on Earth.

The tagging system has some other weirdnesses though. For example nonhazardous memes are still tagged "cognitohazard" for some reason.

item.title: Re: From the article....
item.content:encoded:
If they identified the organisms as 'terrestrial,' they can't be extraterrestrial, right? Or does the initial suspicion of aliens qualify it for the 'extraterrestrial' tag? Nevermind

From the tag guide,

extraterrestrial - SCP originates or is found in outer space, and/or enables or exhibits extraterrestrial travel.

It qualifies as "found in outer space." It's very weird (to me) that it's both terrestrial and extraterrestrial.

item.title: From the article....
item.content:encoded: A radio signal from the station was discovered by the Big Ear radio telescope in 1998, and subsequently intercepted by the Foundation. The signal was initially assumed to be alien in origin, until Foundation astronauts were able to enter the craft and study the preserved organisms, identifying them as terrestrial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this tagged 'extraterrestrial?'

item.title: Re: Actually serious this time.
item.content:encoded: Did you ever write that tale, and if so could we have a link?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eeeh… I didn't feel that way at all. The problem with simplifying the language (further than it already is) is that it actually starts to lose important meaning.

Taking your example as.. Uh.. An example, what part of a starfish would you describe as the 'front?' 'Anterior' is a pretty commonly-known term which, in the case of unfamiliar creatures, is much more useful than any alternative. That's why it's used in scientific contexts, and I presume that's why it's used in this article.

For those readers who don't understand the language necessary to describe the subject, the author has already done something quite helpful: the footnote describes a widely-known similar creature (starfish) as a point of reference. This allows anybody to roughly picture the creature in their head. Any further details are described in, y'know, detail, as they should be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that I had too look up too many scientific terms when reading it. Most of the terms used could have been easily replaced with words the average reader would know. For example, instead of anterior you could have just put the front. I might just be an idiot when it comes to the English language and that's why I was so confused, but in the case that Im not just being dumb I'd leave you with this: It's a good SCP, but you wrote it too much like a scientific article. The average Jo might not know what you're writing about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "an advanced space station" seems to be too informal if you ask me.

item.title: Actually serious this time.
item.content:encoded: Okay, now I actually have made several edits to this article, mostly related to making sure the appearance and biology of the creatures is accurately conveyed, to prepare for a tale I'm writing about them. If you notice any glaring scientific errors or grammatical weirdness, please point and laugh at me them out to me so that I can try and fix them.

item.title: Quack Quack Quack
item.content:encoded: I've made some edits to this article to better reflect the modern standards of the site. Quack quack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking at the various people voting on this, and I just realized I self-upvoted this at some point. I assume it must have been an accident or something, though I'm not sure how I could have accidentally upvoted something. Anyway, I've removed it.

item.title: Tragic
item.content:encoded: This one really hits me right in the feels :'(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oxygenated air

That just sounds awkward, and kind of silly. I understand what the author is saying, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you read that story yet? It's an excellent piece of horror. The only part here that really reminded me of ATMOM was the trilateral symmetry, and even then, the Elder Things had pentalateral(? five-lateral? Idunno.) symmetry. The horror here is because of the skips' loneliness; ATMOM's horror is from, well, typical Lovecraft stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The funny thing was I knew nothing about At the Mountains of Madness before I wrote it, but when someone in the forums told me that I was "rehashing" the story, I had to go look it up. It's always frustrating when you accidentally rip somebody off. They were originally even more like the Elder Things, but I changed some stuff around to make them more original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of At the Mountains of Madness. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ignore my comment in the forums.
Ya done good kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for telling a compelling story without anything but action and body language.

That said, I don't find these things terribly interesting on their own (they could literally be any doodad communicating nonverbally and you'd get the same effect), and I think the body of the description needs some tightening. But those addenda are still a worthy effort on their own. Good job for a first skip!

item.title: Re: Love it.
item.content:encoded: You know, I mentioned a narrative similarity between this and SCP-163. That probably goes for communication, as well. The biggest differences are that these are terrestrial and aquatic, as well as there being more than one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything but the stuff you suggested for the Containment Procedures I've added in. The reason I didn't make additions to the containment procedures is that I'm not sure about adding that much detail. Other people have told me that it slows down the article. You make a very good point about feeding though, so I definitely will add that eventually. I wasn't sure how the Foundation could feed them, so I just handwaved it a bit.

Maybe the Foundation could put anaerobic bacteria into the containers for them to filter out of the water?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The aquarist in me feels the need to note that a 20g saltwater tank would be hell to maintain.

Author — filter idea? A live sand bed? It's the closest to "natural" filtration, I think, but idk about what would break down waste without oxygen — everything I know says the bacteria that work that magic need oxygen. I'd say a lot of the issues raised could be solved with "as they were found" type wording.

Lighting wise, bioluminescent usually equals deep water, where that's (nearly) the only light. The rest I think I covered on your draft, but I'm on my phone and will later.

Edit: remember what I said about axolotls? Some people get around the filter problem with frequent smaller water changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The story has the right pacing and heft, but the science needs some real work, because right now it's detracting from the article.

It's not clear what they eat. Terrestrial autotrophic obligate anaerobes still need nutrients of some kind. Carbon dioxide and hydrogen won't even sustain bacteria for long. What kind of light do they need and how much? What temperatures do they tolerate? Do they require any substrate or filtering? A 20-gallon saltwater fish tank has far more detailed requirements than what's laid out in this section. You don't have to provide every detail, but right now it reads as if none of those basic things are even necessary.

Why are we so sure exactly 74 hours of oxygen exposure will kill them? Where does that number even come from? We've only ever had four of these creatures to study, and the only one that died so far was beaten to death.

In the Discovery section, "de-orbit" is not the best term for how they got to Earth. They were in a solar orbit. De-orbiting would drop them into the sun. This is expressed a little more clearly in the Special Containment Procedures section, but I would still not use the term "pulled" from solar orbit to earth orbit, because that implies gravity or gravity technology is being used by the foundation. "Transferred" is a better term for technology that involves good old fashioned rocketry.

As Photosynthetic pointed out to me long ago, phylum (Echinodermata), class, order, and family names are not italicized. In binomial nomenclature, only genus and species are.

I know that plenty of skips don't need detailed, accurate science to be great articles. But as soon as you introduce serious sci-fi to your story (in this case, returning space stations from solar orbit) you set a high bar for yourself. Once you go there, many readers are going to expect you to feed the nerd.

In this case, the story might work better if it were taken in the other direction, and the sci fi were eliminated entirely. You could say we found 'em in a lake, and now we keep 'em in a similar, artificial lake - few readers would bat an eye at that. The strength of this article is in the story of desperate, imprisoned creatures. The feels contribute all the weight to this article. I'd either rework this to leverage that strength, or else seek more feedback on the science tone.

item.title: Love it.
item.content:encoded:
That -4! What a hot head.
The fact that you made these things symapathic is good writing. During the murderer scene I felt like ceasing all bioluminescent activity myself.
I also liked the fact the Foundation treated alien ambassadors like lab samples. One of their few stupid traits.
If some researcher gets approval to put a few linguists on this full time you are going to have to write a novel.
Even if they built coral spaceships they still must know math and these three are astronauts. That would be a good place to start translation efforts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I to understand that the Foundation is using the shock collar in order to prevent further suicide attempts? Poor things.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^ Partly that, and partly because trying to escape from the the future-apes' laboratory and going mad from confinement is an entirely different thing from "if it seez someone wearin a orange hat et kills everybodyz. Also whenefer it feels liek raging."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I'm not going to be the one to break it to Lord Blackwood, but sea slugs are hermaphrodites. So I doubt sex applies here. Of course, sea slugs don't build spaceships any more than parrots do, so I don't know where the similarities to their modern cousins end.

Also, I think Scorpion was commenting on the scip not copping out by saying "entered a rage state" but actually described wtf happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, -2 and -4 do have rage states… but they're more like actions of depressed and desperate people than typical d-list movie monsters. I'm guessing those two were male, but they're weird plant people, so maybe sex doesn't apply.

Also, author, these are sapient, not just sentient. Parrots are sentient. That doesn't mean they can build spaceships.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember, the ways and order in which humans went about learning to build stuff isn't the only way to do things. There's other ways to work metal than fire forging, for instance- electroplating, acid etching, cold forging, chemical deposition… Really, once they had a nascient industrial base built up they could start using things like geothermal heat from deep sea vents, electric heating elements, or thermite-style self-oxidizing reagents to build hot forges, too.

To make radio waves: Obtain oscillating source of electric current. Attach conductive stick, bowl, or wire (aka antenna or transceiver dish). Ability to control frequency or amplitude of oscillation to fine tune signal optional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cyclopian, this gets an upvote from me in part just for making a cool critter that does not involve the phrases "rage state" or "killed ███ personel".

The rest is for making me empathize with the entities. I think you could push this a bit further, even, by giving us some insight into the reactions of the remaining two sane(or at least less insane) creatures- do they mourn for the dead entity? Comfort each other? Does it drive a wedge between them, and cause them to become hostile to each other as well? All sorts of directions you could go there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooh! Me likey likey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But does it have to be metal? Corals can do some impressive things, and leave behind exoskeletons when they die. We assume technology means metal, but why couldn't things be built of rock and calcium deposits?

That'd hide them better too, if their tech looked like odd rocks. And as for radio signals, make that somehow work like electric eels do? No clue how to turn electricity in radio waves, but you can get electricity out of aquatic beasties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to give the implication that they were wiped out by the GOE, seeing as they're methane breathers that lived a little over two billion years ago. As for technology, hmmmm… That actually made me think of something that could completely undue the article - For any sort of advanced technology you need refined metal, for refined metal you need to be able to smelt metal, to smelt metal you need fire, and for fire you need to not live underwater. Maybe I should add some sort of hint of extraterrestrial uplifting or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for bringing up incident C, actually. People on the forums were telling me that the incident reports were dragging on a bit, so I deleted some stuff from them to make them shorter, including all of -C. I must have forgotten to change the reports beneath it when I deleted it, but the letters are fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was not badly written but I didn't feel anything as a reader. The lack of Accident Log C also felt like it needed more context than the line in -D about containment procedure changes. No vote for now but I may give this another reading in a couple days to see if I can appreciate the characterizations of the organisms more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it certainly reads like a scientific document. That cuts both ways on this article; it's detailed enough to induce that pleasing "this could totally be a real-world writeup of something" feeling while reading something, however it's also excessively detailed for significant stretches. I felt like the containment procedures were a bit of a slog, and the number of times these things ram themselves against an aquarium and the exquisite detail in which those incidents are related is a bit oppressive.

The idea at the article's core is, I think, fairly interesting. Previously dominant species of extremely weird creatures escapes an extinction event and lies in stasis for millions of years, only to be captured, experimented upon, and eventually driven mad by what they must view to be nightmarish monsters. The fact that we cannot communicate with them actually helps to underscore how incomprehensible this all must be for them, which is terrifying to contemplate.

I feel, though, that this previously dominant species and the ramifications of their existence aren't really adequately dealt with. Given the rest of this article, I don't need an extensive history or even any real explanation of these things. But some kind of hints around the edges on this, at least recognizing the fact that there would be an asston of unanswered questions (why aren't these in the fossil record? what happened to all traces of their civilization? is their technology superior or fundamentally different than ours, given their spaceflight capabilities?), would be good. The way the article is written now, there's no real accounting for how monumental a discovery like this would be.

Also, I'm not sure this really affects my vote per se, but the vision of a Foundation that can routinely hijack sophisticated space observation laboratories and send people into space clandestinely stretches my suspension of disbelief a bit. Those are unbelievably complex and difficult activities for any organization, much less a clandestine one. That's kind of of a matter of my own personal vision, but I do think there are logical arguments to be made why that's unlikely.

It's not that far from an upvote, really, but a few of the moving parts in this are miscalibrated, and in a narrative as dry as this one, those all have to be working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And you my +1 (but you knew that already)

:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of pre-human intelligence, and like even more the idea of survivors. However, this does little with the idea, except maybe, "They have a lunatic in their midst."

This reads like a weird mishmash of SCP-163, SCP-1342, SCP-1000. It's far from the worst skip I've ever read, but perhaps a little too ambitious for a first. Very tentative upvote. Welcome to the overambition club, friend.

Edit: Dude, your first skip got an 'almost upvote' from Kalinin. Mad props.

item.title: Good
item.content:encoded: Okay that's good, awesome scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The XXXXs should be fixed now.

item.title: The oldest organisms
item.content:encoded: This is very interesting, these Scp's could hold a key to our evolution, +1 it's awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a few SCP-XXXX's but its alright. +1 i'm my oppinion
